title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 10 — Ma Teng Serves His Country Well / Cao Cao Avenges His Father’s Murder
text:
Li JueandGuo Siwere just about to murderEmperor Xian.

Yet their followersZhang JiandFan Chouargued against it, saying, “You cannot; were you to kill him this very day, we fear the people would not submit to you. Better to first act as though you seek to support the old regime in order to lure the lords of the land into coming into the capital region. If you take the time to remove the Emperor’s supporters before killing him, then the whole realm could be yours.”

Li JueandGuo Sithus followed their advice and had their troops halt. ThenEmperor Xiancalled down to them from the tower, asking, “SinceWang Yunhas already been executed, why has the army not been withdrawn?”

Li JueandGuo Sireplied, “As your servants have done good service on behalf of the royal house, we dare not withdraw the army before we have acknowledged Your Majesty’s titles for us.”

“And what titles are you expecting to receive?” askedEmperor Xian.

The four of them each wrote out their current positions and sent up the list toEmperor Xian, with the understanding that they would now be appointed to comparable positions. Having no choice,Emperor Xianmade the following appointments:

Li Juewas appointed asGeneral of Chariots and Cavalry, Lord ofChiyang county, and acting Commander of the Capital District whileGuo Siwas appointed asGeneral of the Rearand Lord ofMeiyang county, and both of them were granted Military Insignia and were empowered to manipulate both the court and the government as they pleased.

Fan Chouwas appointed asGeneral of the Rightand Lord ofWannian county.

Zhang Jiwas appointed asGeneral of Coursersand Lord ofPingyang commanderyand was assigned to command the soldiers camped atHongnong.

And such people asLi MengandWang Fang, who had opened the city gates for them, were each appointed as Colonels aswell.1•••

Guo Si’s appointment as “Lord of Meiyang” seems to be missing from the original text.↩

Having thankedEmperor Xianfor his generosity,Li Jueand the others led their troops out of the city.

They also sent out orders to search for whatever was left ofDong Zhuo’s corpse and were able to obtain a few remaining fragments of his skin and bones, and they carved an image of fragrant wood in his likeness; once all was in readiness, they made a great show of offering sacrifices, they dressed the image in a royal cap and gown and laid it in a royal coffin and casket, and they selected an auspicious day to lead an official funeral procession to bring the remains to be buried atMeiwu castle.

Yet just when they were about to bury the casket, a great thunder storm came down from the heavens, flooding the flat ground with several feet of water, and a bolt of lightning burst the casket open and sent the body flying out of it.Li Juewaited for the weather to clear and attempted another burial, but that night the same thing happened again. Nor did a third attempt prove any more successful. For indeed, by now even the pitiful traces of skin and bone had themselves been entirely consumed by the blaze of the lightning. Severe indeed could one describe Heaven’s anger towardsDong Zhuo!

Now that they had all the power of the government in their grasp,Li JueandGuo Siinflicted cruel violence against the common people, and they secretly sent their own agents to stand attendance uponEmperor Xianin order to keep an eye on his every movement, greatly constraining what he could do. All promotions or demotions among the court ministers and the government officials were according to the whims of these two miscreants, though in order to appeal to popular sentiment they made a special show of invitingZhu Juninto the government, appointing him asMinister Chamberlainand bringing him into their councils.

One day came a report that theGrand AdministratorofLiang province,Ma Teng, and the Inspector of Bingzhou,Han Sui, were rapidly approaching Chang’an at the head of more than a hundred thousand soldiers, declaring an intention of attacking the rebels.

In fact these two had already sent an agent into the capital in order to conspire with ministers who would support them from within againstLi Jueand his partizans and had gained the support of a Palace Attendant,Ma Yu, a Counselor-Remonstrant,Chong Shao, and the General of the Household Gentlemen of the Left,Liu Fan. The three of them had even managed to privately informEmperor Xianof the plot and had sent off two copies of a secret edict toMa TengandHan Sui, appointing the one asGeneral who Conquers the Westand the other asGeneral who Guards the Westand charging them to combine their strength to vanquish the traitors.

When they heard of the approach of these two generals,Li Jue,Guo Si,Zhang Ji, andFan Chouargued among themselves how to meet the attack. Their adviserJia Xuproposed, “As the two generals are having to travel a long distance, we should simply deepen our ditches and raise our ramparts, mounting a stout defense to resist them. In less than a hundred days, their supplies will be consumed and they will have to retire. Then we can lead out our troops to pursue them, and we can be sure of capturing them.”

ButLi MengandWang Fangobjected, “This is no good plan. Just lend us ten thousand good men and we’ll take the heads ofMa TengandHan Suiright where they stand, then present them to you under your own banners.”

Jia Xusaid, “If you fight them immediately, defeat is certain.”

The other two exclaimed, “If we fail, we’re ready to lose our heads. But if we win the battle, Sir, you ought to give us yours.”

Jia Xuthen remarked toLi JueandGuo Si, “Two hundred li west of Chang’an stand theZhouzhi Hills, where the road is narrow and difficult. It wouldn’t be a bad idea to send Generals Zhang and Fan to camp troops there and build firm ramparts to defend it whileLi MengandWang Fangare leading their own troops out to face the enemy.” They did as he advised.

Once fifteen thousand soldiers had been assigned to the command ofLi MengandWang Fang, the two of them left in high hopes, making their camp two hundred and eighty li fromChang’an.

Soon the soldiers from Xiliang arrived, and the duo led their troops out to face them. As the Xiliang soldiers blocked the road and barred the way with their formation,Ma TengandHan Suirode out ahead together. They pointed atLi MengandWang Fangand abused them, saying, “Rebels against the state! Who will go and capture them?”

Hardly had the words been spoken when a youthful officer dashed out of the western lines; he had a face clear as the purest jade and eyes like shooting stars, he had the torso of a tiger and the arms of an ape, and he wielded a long spear and sat astride an excellent steed. It wasMa Teng’s own sonMa Chao, styledMengqi, and though he was but sixteen years old, none could match him for boldness.

Thinking little of his young opponent,Wang Fanggalloped forth to challenge him. Yet within just a few bouts,Wang Fanghad already been sent to the ground by a single thrust fromMa Chao, who turned to ride back again. But at the sight ofWang Fang’s skeweringLi Menghad rode out on his own to chase afterMa Chao, who appeared to be none the wiser. From beneath the camp gate of his army,Ma Tengyelled to his son, “He’s coming for you!” Yet no sooner had he said this than he saw thatMa Chaohad already grabbedLi Mengand placed him on his own horse.

ForMa Chaohad known thatLi Mengwas following him and only pretended not to see, waiting until his enemy came close and lifted his spear to strike. ThenMa Chaodeftly leaned out of the way, so thatLi Meng’s spear thrust met only empty air, and as the horses passedMa Chao’s powerful arm shot out and pulledLi Mengfrom the saddle. With the loss of bothWang FangandLi Meng, their soldiers saw that victory was hopeless and began to flee, andMa TengandHan Suiseized their advantage to pursue and slay them, winning a great victory. They pressed on to threaten the enemy camp guarding the hills, hanging upLi Meng’s served head to unnerve their foes.

WhenLi JueandGuo Silearned thatLi MengandWang Fanghad both been killed byMa Chao, they finally believed in the clarity ofJia Xu’s foresight and better appreciated his advice. Thus they resolved to hold fast to their defenses and refuse to respond to any further provocations for battle.

Sure enough, in less than two months the supplies of theLiangmen were all exhausted and the leaders began to consider retreat.

And as fate would have it, within the capital a household servant ofMa Yu’s family revealed that his master had been working withLiu FanandChong Shaoto conspire withMa TengandHan Suito aid them from within the city. Furious,Li JueandGuo Siarrested every member of all three families, regardless of age or conduct, and had them beheaded in the marketplace. The heads of these three conspirators were then sent to the front line to serve as a counter-warning.

Since their food had been exhausted and their collaborators within the capital had been exposed,Ma TengandHan Suisaw no choice but to pull up their camp and withdraw their forces.Li JueandGuo SiorderedZhang Jito lead his troops to pursueMa TengandFan Chouto lead his to chaseHan Sui. The Xiliang army was greatly defeated, and only byMa Chao’s desperate fighting in the rearguard wasZhang Jidriven off.

Fan Choukept up a persistent pursuit ofHan Sui. Somewhere near Chencang,Han Suiwheeled his horse around towardsFan Chouand said, “Sir, must you really be so merciless towards one of your own neighbors?”

Fan Choudrew up his horse and replied, “An imperial command is not to be violated!”

“Yet I too was only acting in the service of the state,” saidHan Sui. “Why then do you press me so hard?”

ThusFan Chouagreed to give up the pursuit, and he turned his horse away, called his men back to camp, and letHan Suidepart in peace. Yet he did not know thatLi Jue’s nephew Li Bie had seenFan ChouletHan Suiget away, and Li Bie returned to Chang’an and informed his uncle.Li Juewas so enraged that he wanted to muster his troops and march againstFan Chouthen and there, butJia Xuadvised him, “The people’s hearts are not yet settled, and to engage in further warfare would be most undesirable. Better to prepare a feast and inviteZhang JiandFan Chouto attend to congratulate them on their achievements; you could then grabFan Chouoff his mat and have him beheaded without expending the slightest effort.”Li Juewas very taken with this idea; he promptly organized a feast and invitedZhang JiandFan Chouto come, and they were only too happy to attend. Once everyone was mellow with wine,Li Jue’s expression suddenly darkened as he demanded, “Fan Chou, why were you conspiring withHan Sui? Do you mean to plot treason?”

Fan Chouwas caught completely off guard, and before he could even offer a response he saw the assassins rush out with swords and axes. In a moment all was over and his head lay beneath the table.

Scared beyond measure,Zhang Jigroveled on the floor. ButLi Juehelped him to his feet, telling him, “It was for plotting rebellion thatFan Chouwas executed. You are one of my own confidantes, sir; what cause have you to fear?”

Li JuereassignedFan Chou’s soldiers toZhang Ji’s command, andZhang Jichose to return toHongnong. From this time on, sinceLi JueandGuo Sihad managed to defeat the soldiers of Xiliang, none of the other lords of the land dared to openly oppose them either. At the same time,Jia Xukept urging them to offer comfort and stability to the common people and to seek and accept the services of worthy and influential people. Thus the legitimacy of the imperial court was somewhat restored.

Eventually the court learned of a new development: in Qingzhou yet another group of Yellow Turbans had launched a rebellion, having gathered an army of hundreds of thousands, and they acted without any real leadership and raided and plundered both the gentry and the people.Zhu Junproposed the name of one man who he guaranteed would be able to smash these rebels, and whenLi JueandGuo Siasked who he meant, he told them, “For routing bandits in the lands east of the mountains, no one butCao Mengdewill do.”

“And where isMengdenow?” askedLi Jue.

Zhu Junreplied, “He is serving asGrand AdministratorofDongcommandery and has a large army at his disposal. If you order this man to fight the rebels, they will be broken quick as you please.”

Li Juewas glad to hear this, and overnight a hasty edict was sent out towards Dong commandery, orderingCao Caoto join with the Chancellor of Jibei,Bao Xin, to fight these Yellow Turbans together. As soon asCao Caoreceived the court command, he arranged a meeting withBao Xin, and they raised their troops and attacked the rebels atShouyang.Bao Xincut his way into the enemy lines and, though he was killed by the rebels,Cao Caopressed his assault to chase the enemy all the way back to Jibei, where tens of thousands of them surrendered to him.Cao Caothen placed these former rebels into his own vanguard, and wherever his army went, none did not surrender. Within about a hundred days,Cao Caohad compelled the submission of the enemy: more than three hundred thousand soldiers and more than a million other men and women.

Cao Caoselected the most skilled of these new adherents, calling them the “Qingzhou Troops”, while the others were all sent back home to their fields. Thanks to this victory,Cao Cao’s power and prestige rose by the day. He reported his success to the capital, and the court promoted him asGeneral who Guards the East.

WhileCao Caowas inYan province, he sought out worthy people and welcomed gentlemen. One of these was a native of Yingyin county in Yingchuan commandery:Xun YustyledWenruo, the son ofXun Gun. ThisXun Yuhad previously served underYuan Shao, but at this time he leftYuan Shaoto come serveCao Cao, who was so pleased after speaking with him that he exclaimed, “Here is my own Zifang!” And he employedXun Yuas acting Army Marshal. Another man wasXun Yu’s nephewXun YoustyledGongda; he was already widely respected across the realm and had once been appointed as a Gentleman-Attendant of the Yellow Gates at the capital, but had later abandoned that office to return to his hometown. He had come to joinCao Caotogether with his uncle, andCao Caoappointed him as acting Directing Instructor.

Xun YutoldCao Cao, “I’ve heard thatYan provincehas a certain worthy gentleman, only I do not know where he is now.”

“Whom do you mean?” askedCao Cao.

Xun Yureplied, “Cheng YustyledZhongde, a native of Dong’a county in Dong commandery.”

“I’ve long heard of his name as well,” saidCao Cao. Thus he sent an agent to ask around the area for him, and he was found to be studying texts among the hills.Cao Caoinvited him to come and accept an appointment, and whenCheng Yuarrived and met with him,Cao Caowas quite pleased.

Cheng Yusaid toXun Yu, “Such a crude and obscure fellow as myself was hardly worthy of your recommendation. But there is your neighborGuo JiastyledFengxiao; he would be a model gentleman of our times. Why not spread the net to attract him?”

“I had nearly forgotten!” saidXun Yusuddenly. And he recommended thatCao Caooffer an invitation forGuo Jiato come to Yanzhou, where together they discussed the affairs of the realm. For his part,Guo Jiarecommended a native of Chengde county in Huainan commandery,Liu YestyledZiyang, who was a direct descendant of the founder of Later Han, Emperor Guangwu;Cao Caopromptly invited him as well.Liu Yehad two suggestions of his own: one wasMan ChongstyledBoning, from Changyi county in Shanyang commandery and the other wasLü QianstyledZike, from Wucheng commandery. AsCao Caohad also long known the reputation of these two figures, he invited them to join him by serving as attendant army officers. These two then recommended another man:Mao Jie, styledXiaoxian, of Pingqiu county in Chenliu commandery.Cao Caorecruited him as well as an attendant officer. Then a famous leader, with his troop of some hundreds, arrived to offer service: this wasYu JinstyledWenze, of Juping county inTaishancommandery. WhenCao Caosaw howYu Jinwas skilled at mounted archery and that his martial prowess was exceptional, he appointed him as an army inspector.

Then another day,Xiahou Dunbrought a giant of a man to meetCao Cao.

“And who is this?” askedCao Cao.

Xiahou Dunreplied, “He is fromChenliu; his name isDian Wei. He has uncanny boldness and strength. He was one ofZhang Miao’s men, but quarreled with his tent companions and killed more than a dozen of them with his fists before fleeing into the hills. When I was out hunting, I found him chasing a deer across a stream, so I took him into custody in my army. Now I’m specially presenting him to you.”

“A man who looks as imposing as he does,” remarkedCao Cao, “cannot lack for boldness or strength.”

Xiahou Dunreplied, “He once killed a man once to avenge a friend and carried his head through the whole market place; hundreds saw him, but dared not come near. And the weapon he uses now is a two-branched iron halberd, weighing eighty catties, yet when he is sitting in the saddle he can make it fly.”

Cao CaobadeDian Weishow him, andDian Weiwielded his halberd while riding about, dashing to and fro. Then he saw away among the tents a huge banner, swaying dangerously in the force of the wind and on the point of falling, with a crowd of soldiers vainly struggling to keep it steady.Dian Weidismounted and shouted to the men to clear out, and with one hand he held the staff quite firm, keeping it perfectly upright in spite of the strong wind.

“This is oldElaicome again!” saidCao Cao. He appointedDian Weias his Commandant Before The Tent, and he even took off the brocade coat he was wearing and gave it as a gift toDian Weialong with a fine steed and a handsome saddle.

Thus amongCao Cao’s subordinates there were cunning ministers on the civil side and fierce generals on the military one, and his might became known all through the lands east of the mountains.

Sometime earlier, seeking to avoid the rising turmoil,Cao Cao’s fatherCao Songhad left their home region in Chenliu to live incognito inLangyecommandery. At this time,Cao Caosent theGrand AdministratorofTaishan,Ying Shao, to travel to Langye to escort his father toYan province. The very day thatCao Songreceived his son’s letter, he at once set out for Yanzhou along with his younger brotherCao Deand all the members of his household, more than forty people young and old. They led a procession of more than a hundred carts and were accompanied by more than a hundred porters.

Their road led throughXu province, whoseGrand Administrator,Tao QianstyledGongzu, was warm and generous, pure and honest; he had long wished to get on good terms withCao Cao, and had only lacked the means to do so. Hearing thatCao Cao’s father was passing through his district, he came out to the border to welcome and receive them, bowing twice beforeCao Songto show the extent of his respect, and he prepared a great feast and for two days offered them his hospitality. And whenCao Songwas about to leave, not only didTao Qianaccompany him out of the city, he even assigned his own Commandant,Zhang Kai, to lead five hundred ofTao Qian’s soldiers to escort him the rest of the way.

However,Cao Songhad only led his family a short distance further when, somewhere between the counties of Hua and Fei, one of those storms that are so rampant during the passing of summer and the coming of autumn happened to burst overhead, and the procession was obliged to seek lodging in the grounds of an old temple. As the monks led the party inside,Cao Songhad his household help itself to the temple proper while orderingZhang Kaito put up his soldiers in the two outbuildings. The clothing and gear of the Xuzhou soldiers had all been battered and soaked by the rain, and they were grumbling one and all. ThusZhang Kaitook his chief subordinates aside to some quiet spot and told them, “We’re just a bunch of washed-up Yellow Turbans who were coerced into submitting toTao Qian, and there’s been nothing for it. Now here’s thisCao Songwith his endless carts of goods; if you fellows want wealth and prestige, it’ll be no trouble. Just wait until the third watch tonight and then we’ll all cut our way in, killCao Songand his whole family, take his wealth and goods, and then run off into the wild. What do you think?”

His minions all agreed. That night, before the wind and the rain had died down and whileCao Songwas sitting in the main chamber, he suddenly heard a clamor of voices all around the walls.Cao Dedrew his sword and went out to look, but was suddenly stabbed to death. Terrified,Cao Songled a concubine to flee into the rear yard, hoping to scale the wall and get away, but the lady was too heavy and could not get over it. Out of desperation,Cao Songand the concubine hid in a lavatory, but they were still killed by the marauding soldiers.

Ying Shaoran for his life and managed to get away, and he went to take refuge underYuan Shao. OnceZhang Kaihad killedCao Song’s whole household and pilfered his wealth, he set fire to the temple and burned it down, then fled to Huainan with five hundred followers.

A poet of a later age penned a few lines aboutCao Cao:

He justified his butchery

With “better they should die than me”

Thus Heaven sent the bandit’s blade

To see his debt of blood repaid

Some ofYing Shao’s soldiers who had escaped took the evil tidings toCao Cao. When he heard it, he fell to the earth with a great cry. As others helped him back to his feet, through his gnashing teethCao Caodeclared, “Tao Qianlet his soldiers kill my father; I will not share the same sky with such a foe! I will muster my whole army at once and sweep Xuzhou off the face of the earth. Only thus can I slake my hatred!”

And he left only thirty thousand soldiers underXun YuandCheng Yuto guard the counties of Juancheng, Fan, and Dong’a, while leading all the rest of his troops on a wild rampage towards Xuzhou, withXiahou Dun,Yu Jin, andDian Weileading the vanguard.Cao Caoordered that for the sake of vengeance against his father’s foe, as soon as Xuzhou’s defenses were overcome it was to be sacked and put to the sword, leaving no one alive.

Now theGrand AdministratorofJiujiang,Bian Rang, was a close friend ofTao Qian. Hearing that Xuzhou was threatened, he set out to his friend’s aid with five thousand soldiers. ButCao Caowas enraged when he learned of this; he sentXiahou Dunto interceptBian Rang, andXiahou Dunkilled him.

By this time,Cao Cao’s former companionChen Gonghad been appointed as Attendant Officer of Dong commandery and had also formed a friendship withTao Qian. Hearing thatCao Caohad raised troops for vengeance and was planning to massacre the people,Chen Gonghurried through the night to come see him. Although at firstCao Caowanted to put him off, knowing full well thatChen Gongwas only going to persuade him to spareTao Qian, he could not deny the past kindness thatChen Gonghad shown him and in the end he invitedChen Gonginto his tent for a meeting.

Chen Gongsaid, “Your Excellency, I had heard that in bringing your grand army to Xuzhou in order to avenge your esteemed father you are planning to slaughter the people, thus I came all this way to offer my advice.Tao Qianis humane and a good man, not the sort who craves profit and forgets righteousness; the villainy thatZhang Kaihas committed was notTao Qian’s offense. Furthermore, what have the people of Xuzhou and its counties done to deserve Your Excellency’s ire? To kill them would invite misfortune. May you ‘think thrice’ before proceeding.”

ButCao Caospat back, “You already left me to my own devices once before, Sir, yet you have the gall to come face me now?Tao Qiankilled my whole family; I swear I will pull out his guts and carve out his heart to slake my hatred! And though you may have come to plead on his behalf, if I refuse to listen, what are you going to do about it?”

After taking his leave and walking out,Chen Gonglamented, “I don’t deserve to faceTao Qianeither!” And he rode off to take refuge under theGrand AdministratorofChenliu,Zhang Miao.

WhereverCao Cao’s vast army went, they butchered the people and dug up their graves and tombs.

WhenTao Qianat Xuzhou heard thatCao Caohad raised an army seeking vengeance and was slaughtering the people, he lifted his head towards the heavens and trembled in grief, saying, “I must have committed some sin against Heaven, that the people of Xuzhou now endure such a hardship!” And he quickly called together his officials to consult with them. One of them,Cao Bao, said, “When the Cao troops are nearly upon us, how can we just sit here with folded hands and waiting to die? Commissioner, I for one will help you to make a fight.”

Tao Qiancould only lead out his troops to confront the enemy. In the distance they could seeCao Cao’s army covering the field like a layer of frost or a flurry of snow, and in their midst was a raised white banner, upon which the words Vengeance and Vendetta were writ large on both sides.

Once his formation was all in order for battle,Cao Caorode out ahead, dressed in mourning garb and waving his whip as he vented his spleen.Tao Qiantoo rode out to pause beneath the banner at his camp gate, drawing himself up to extend his courtesies. “Your Excellency,” he said, “it was purely because I sought your good graces that I entrustedZhang Kaiwith guarding and escorting your father; it was only my failure to realize that his wicked heart had not changed that led to what happened. This was never my intention; may Your Excellency study this further.”

ButCao Caoonly yelled back, “Codger! You killed my father and yet you still dare to make excuses! Who can capture that traitorous geezer?”

Xiahou Dunrode out at once in response, andTao Qianfrightfully hurried back behind his formation. AsXiahou Dunrushed forward,Cao Baoset his spear and galloped out to face him. Yet just as the two horses were drawing up to each other, a wild flurry arose, flinging so much sand and scattering so much dust into the air that both armies fell into confusion. Each side fell back to reform their lines.

Tao Qianwent back into the city and gathered his ministers for another council. “The Cao army is so strong and vast that it would be hard to match them,” he said. “I ought to tie myself up and go toCao Cao’s camp, letting him cut me up as he likes, in order to spare the lives of the people of Xuzhou.”

But the words were hardly out of his mouth before someone else stepped forward to say, “Commissioner, you have served at Xuzhou for a long time, and both the gentry and the people have been moved by your kindness. Though the Cao army is vast, it cannot necessarily take the city. You should work together with the people to hold fast to our defenses rather than lead the army out again. And though I am of little talent, I am prepared to carry out a little ploy to teachCao Caowhat it’s like to have no home to return to!”

The others were so taken aback that they at once inquired what strategy he proposed.

And so it was:

Hoping for love, he got hate instead

When all seemed lost, how’d he save his head?

Who was the speaker? The next chapter will explain.
